skill.name: when-debugging-code-use-debugging-assistant
skill.description: A structured debugging workflow for diagnosing code failures, finding root causes, proposing fixes, validating tests, and preventing regressions.
skill.summary: Guide code debugging from symptoms to validated fixes.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and Ruby backtick hits are documentation or schema false positives. The confirmed concern is the executable slash command, which invokes external Claude Flow commands and uses eval on a configurable test command; publish only with a warning and user review guidance.

medium_findings.title: Configurable command executed with eval
medium_findings.description: The script loads `testCommand` from `.debug-config.json` and runs it with `eval`. This is legitimate for test automation, but unsafe in untrusted repositories because local configuration can execute arbitrary shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The config value is read from a project file and later passed directly to eval. This confirms a command execution risk, although the purpose is test execution rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: External package commands receive debugging context
medium_findings.description: The script invokes `npx claude-flow@alpha` hooks and memory commands, including issue descriptions, log excerpts, and recent git history. This creates dependency and data handling risk if users run it with sensitive debugging material.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct npx execution is present and later memory store calls receive gathered context. The destination behavior depends on Claude Flow, so this is a high-confidence operational risk but not confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Optional log file contents are collected into session context
medium_findings.description: When a log path is supplied, the script reads the last 50 lines and stores them in debugging context. Logs may contain secrets, tokens, or user data, so users need an explicit warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read is visible and purposeful for debugging. Risk depends on user-provided log contents and downstream handling by Claude Flow memory commands.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: Representative flagged lines describe debugging metadata or ordinary variables, not cryptographic algorithms or hashing operations. No evidence found of weak crypto implementation in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited JSON lines contain the tool name and description. Static matches appear to be keyword collisions rather than actual crypto use.

low_findings.title: Markdown backtick execution alerts are mostly examples
low_findings.description: Many Ruby or shell backtick findings occur inside Markdown fences showing commands or data formats. These examples are inert documentation unless a user manually copies them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are a fenced quick-start example. They are not executed by the skill at runtime.

low_findings.title: Environment access alert is a documentation sample
low_findings.description: The `process.env.NODE_ENV` occurrence is part of a JavaScript prevention example in PROCESS.md. It does not read secrets or execute in the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code appears inside a documentation example about assertions. No evidence found that this file is executed.

low_findings.title: System and network reconnaissance alerts are workflow labels
low_findings.description: Representative process diagram labels such as memory flow and testing steps are descriptions of debugging phases. No evidence found of network scanning or reconnaissance behavior in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Graphviz edge labels for validation outcomes. They do not invoke network or system reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configurable command executed with eval
dangerous_patterns.description: The script loads `testCommand` from `.debug-config.json` and runs it with `eval`. This is legitimate for test automation, but unsafe in untrusted repositories because local configuration can execute arbitrary shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The config value is read from a project file and later passed directly to eval. This confirms a command execution risk, although the purpose is test execution rather than confirmed malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: External package commands receive debugging context
dangerous_patterns.description: The script invokes `npx claude-flow@alpha` hooks and memory commands, including issue descriptions, log excerpts, and recent git history. This creates dependency and data handling risk if users run it with sensitive debugging material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct npx execution is present and later memory store calls receive gathered context. The destination behavior depends on Claude Flow, so this is a high-confidence operational risk but not confirmed exfiltration.

content.user_title: Debug Code with a Structured Workflow
content.value_statement: Debugging often fails when symptoms, causes, fixes, and tests are mixed together. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a phased workflow for reliable investigation and validation.
actual_capabilities: Organizes debugging into symptom identification, root cause analysis, fix generation, validation testing, and regression prevention.
Provides a slash command script for starting and tracking debugging sessions.
Defines an MCP-style input and output schema for debugging requests and results.
Documents agent roles for code analysis, coding, and testing phases.
Includes examples for null errors, race conditions, memory leaks, async issues, and performance regressions.
Can collect recent git context and optional log excerpts during a debugging session.
limitations: The workflow depends on project access, reproducible symptoms, and available test commands.
The slash command script calls Claude Flow tooling and may require network or package execution permissions.
Automatic validation uses a configurable shell command, which must be reviewed before use.
The skill provides workflow guidance and placeholders; it does not guarantee a correct fix.

use_cases.title: Triage production regressions
use_cases.description: Use the phases to collect symptoms, inspect recent changes, and produce a focused root cause summary.

use_cases.title: Fix failing test suites
use_cases.description: Guide an assistant through reproducing the failure, selecting a fix, and validating that tests pass.

use_cases.title: Standardize debugging reviews
use_cases.description: Use the report structure to document evidence, alternatives, validation results, and prevention steps.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic debug session
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the debugging assistant workflow for this issue: [describe the error]. Ask for missing reproduction details before proposing a fix.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a failing test
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the five-phase debugging process to this failing test output: [paste output]. Identify the likely root cause and validation plan.

prompt_templates.title: Compare fix options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the debugging assistant to compare two or three fixes for this bug: [context]. Explain tradeoffs, risks, and required tests.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a regression prevention plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the debugging assistant workflow after this incident: [summary]. Create prevention steps, monitoring ideas, and review checklist items.

output_examples.input: Login fails with an undefined user error after a recent async refactor.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a symptom summary, a likely missing await root cause, a validation test plan, and prevention steps for async handling.

output_examples.input: Checkout tests fail intermittently under parallel execution.
output_examples.output: The assistant separates timing symptoms from data state, proposes race condition hypotheses, and recommends targeted concurrency tests.

output_examples.input: API latency increased after a deployment.
output_examples.output: The assistant frames a performance investigation, checks recent changes, suggests profiling evidence, and defines validation metrics.

best_practices: Review `.debug-config.json` before running automatic validation commands.
Provide exact error messages, reproduction steps, recent changes, and environment details.
Require a failing test or clear reproduction before accepting a proposed fix.
anti_patterns: Do not run the slash command in untrusted repositories without reviewing local configuration.
Do not paste secrets, tokens, or private customer data into logs or issue descriptions.
Do not skip validation because a proposed root cause sounds plausible.

faq.question: Does this skill change code automatically?
faq.answer: The documents describe fix generation and validation. Actual edits depend on the assistant and tools used with the workflow.

faq.question: Can it run tests?
faq.answer: The slash command can run a configured test command when automatic testing is enabled.

faq.question: What tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It references Claude Flow hooks, memory commands, and agents for code analysis, coding, and testing.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The script can pass issue descriptions, log excerpts, and git context to Claude Flow commands.

faq.question: What issues fit this workflow best?
faq.answer: It fits runtime errors, failing tests, race conditions, performance regressions, memory leaks, and integration failures.

faq.question: What should I review before use?
faq.answer: Review the slash command script, `.debug-config.json`, log file paths, and any package execution permissions.
